category_title: Young immigrant boys labeled as 'struggling readers' with non-normative literacy behaviors
minimal_context: The student is gathered on the rug at the beginning of an after-school program session to listen to a read aloud of Visiting Day and Chrysanthemum.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Welcome to the after-school program. Let's gather on the rug so I can read Visiting Day.

STUDENT: (comes closer and points to the picture) Oh, he’s wearing jail clothes!

TUTOR: Please sit in your designated rug spot.

STUDENT: (talking out of turn and turning the page) In the back page there was cup, brushes in his room. Se:::e? He is living there. He brushes his teeth there and he …

TUTOR: We need to sit still. I am going to read Chrysanthemum instead before we begin.

STUDENT: (sighs heavily, slumps over, turns back away, and averts gaze) I’ve already heard it four times!

options.A: Welcome the student's responses and enthusiasm, stepping back to allow them to engage in meaningful conversation about the text and paying attention to what they say.
options.B: Ask a summarizing question to highlight the gravity of the point the student made about the text (e.g., 'So, he is living there?').
options.C: Joke with the student about the text and laugh at oneself during the read-aloud session on the rug.
options.D: Use recasts by repeating the student's incorrect grammatical usage correctly during the read-aloud session on the rug.
